text: Physical stimuli-responsive strategies lack controllability, predictability, feasibility, and standardization in bone repair and regeneration applications according to existing research. These materials may cause damage to bone tissue, thereby affecting bone regeneration $ ^{1} $. However, physical stimuli-responsive strategies still have broad application prospects in bone repair and regeneration. For example, physical stimuli-responsive strategies can affect cell behavior by adjusting the physical properties of the extracellular matrix, thereby promoting bone repair and regeneration. In addition, physical stimuli-responsive strategies can be combined with other treatments such as drug therapy and gene therapy to improve treatment efficacy. With the development of technology, the development of physical stimuli-responsive materials will be a hot spot and new direction for the development of orthopedic biomaterials in the next decade. Multidisciplinary cross-fusion is also an inevitable trend in the clinical development of orthopedics. Therefore, future research will focus on developing better materials to promote bone tissue regeneration and functional reconstruction.

text: Understanding the molecular mechanism of bioactive materials on bone tissue can improve the design and preparation of materials to better promote bone regeneration. By studying the molecular mechanism, we can discover the interaction between the material and cells and tissues, so as to optimize the biological activity of the material and improve the effect of bone regeneration. Different bioactive materials may have different molecular mechanisms for bone regeneration. Through in-depth study of molecular mechanisms, we can understand the characteristics and application range of different materials, so as to guide the appropriate material selection and application. This helps to improve the success rate and treatment effect of bone tissue engineering.

text: 4.1. Regulation of Stem Cells by Biomaterials
evidence: paragraph_title label with numbering: 4.1. Regulation of Stem Cells by Biomaterials
seed_evidence: paragraph_title label with numbering: 4.1. Regulation of Stem Cells by Biomaterials

raw_observation.text: 4.1. Regulation of Stem Cells by Biomaterials

marker_signature.normalized_text: 4.1. Regulation of Stem Cells by Biomaterials

text: Stem cells are undifferentiated cells that self-renew and produce an abundance of undifferentiated offspring. They have strong proliferative capacity and multi-lineage differentiation ability; therefore, they play an important role in tissue regeneration. Embryonic stem cells (ESCs) have self-renewal ability and pluripotency and can differentiate into various cell types, including the capacity to form osteogenic cells. $ ^{[167,168]} $ Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) possess limitless proliferation and pluripotent differentiation potential with no ethical issues and are thus regarded as a promising option for osteogenesis and bone repair. iPSCs are capable of differentiating into osteoblasts. $ ^{[169]} $ MSCs with outstanding proliferation and differentiation potential are multipotent stromal cells that can differentiate into several cell types, including osteoblasts, chondrocytes, adipocytes, myocytes, and endothelial cells. MSCs have immunomodulatory functions and retain the features of stem cells after multiple passages without immune rejection.

raw_observation.text: Stem cells are undifferentiated cells that self-renew and produce an abundance of undifferentiated offspring. They have strong proliferative capacity and multi-lineage differentiation ability; therefore, they play an important role in tissue regeneration. Embryonic stem cells (ESCs) have self-renewal ability and pluripotency and can differentiate into various cell types, including the capacity to form osteogenic cells. $ ^{[167,168]} $ Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) possess limitless proliferation and pluripotent differentiation potential with no ethical issues and are thus regarded as a promising option for osteogenesis and bone repair. iPSCs are capable of differentiating into osteoblasts. $ ^{[169]} $ MSCs with outstanding proliferation and differentiation potential are multipotent stromal cells that can differentiate into several cell types, including osteoblasts, chondrocytes, adipocytes, myocytes, and endothelial cells. MSCs have immunomodulatory functions and retain the features of stem cells after multiple passages without immune rejection.

MSCs have immunomodulatory functions and retain the features of stem cells after multiple passages without immune rejection.

text: Bone biomaterials act as platforms to facilitate stem cell adhesion and growth during bone repair. The chemical components, surface characteristics, and topographic structure of bone biomaterials directly affect stem cell adhesion, proliferation, differentiation, and angiogenesis. Various attempts have been made to combine scaffolds and stem cells for bone regeneration. Human ESC-derived MSCs encapsulated in alginate microbeads in macroporous CaP cement exhibit good cytocompatibility, osteogenesis, and mineralization. $ ^{[170]} $ ECM-coated PLGA scaffolds enhanced the proliferation of ESCs, calcium deposition, and osteocalcin expression. $ ^{[171]} $ Poly(L-lactic acid) (PLLA) loaded with calcium-deficient nanohydroxyapatite (d-HAP) promotes the expression of osteogenic markers in iPSCs compared to pure PLLA. $ ^{[172]} $ Gingival MSC-laden alginate-based hydrogels with controllable mechanical properties exhibited good biocompatibility, biodegradability, and osteoconductivity after subcutaneous implantation in mice and facilitated craniofacial bone regeneration. $ ^{[173]} $ A composite of bone marrow-derived stromal cells and  $ \beta $-TCP transplanted into a monkey femoral defect exhibited enhanced bone regeneration compared to  $ \beta $-TCP alone. $ ^{[174]} $ A chitosan scaffold combined with osteogenically induced bone marrow MSCs promoted the formation of new bone and collagen in the pores of the scaffold and showed excellent biodegradability. $ ^{[175]} $

text: Several signaling pathways and transcription factors have been shown to regulate the osteogenic differentiation of stem cells. To develop novel strategies for bone repair and regeneration, it is necessary to fully understand the underlying molecular mechanisms associated with the interactions between bone biomaterials and stem cells.

text: 4.2.1. BMP Signaling Pathway

raw_observation.text: 4.2.1. BMP Signaling Pathway

marker_signature.normalized_text: 4.2.1. BMP Signaling Pathway

text: BMPs belong to the TGF- $ \beta $ superfamily and are of great importance in bone formation and tissue homeostasis. This signaling is initiated by the binding of BMP to BMP receptors on the cell membrane, and the downstream Smad proteins are then phosphorylated by activated receptors. Smad complexes translocate into the nucleus, activating osteogenesis-related transcription factors and their corresponding downstream targets. BMPs regulate stem cell growth and differentiation and accelerate stem cell osteogenic differentiation. BMP-2, 4, 6, 7, and 9 effectively improve osteogenic differentiation in MSCs and induce new bone formation. Several biomaterials have been shown to activate the BMP signaling pathway. Nanofibrous HAP/chitosan nanocomposite scaffolds significantly promoted the proliferation and adhesion of MSCs, and the composite upregulated the expression of Smad1, BMP-2/4, Runx2, ALP, collagen I, integrin subunits, and myosins. Furthermore, the key proteins in the BMP signaling pathway, pSmad1/5/8, exhibited clear nuclear localization, and osteocalcin was significantly increased on the scaffold. Nanofibrous HAP/chitosan scaffolds combined with MSCs improved bone regeneration in cranial bone defects by initiating the integrin-BMP/Smad pathway. $ ^{[6]} $ Nanostructured titanium discs treated with  $ H_{2}SO_{4}/H_{2}O_{2} $ promote osteoblast differentiation in osteoblastic cells by increasing the endogenous secretion of BMP-2 and regulating the BMP pathway. $ ^{[176]} $ BMP2-modified black phosphorus (BP@BMP2) nanosheets to PLLA electrospun fibrous scaffold, with a combination of recruiting osteoblast precursor cells and biomineralization properties for bone regeneration (Figure 8A,B). $ ^{[177]} $

text: 4.2.2. Wnt Signaling Pathway

raw_observation.text: 4.2.2. Wnt Signaling Pathway

marker_signature.normalized_text: 4.2.2. Wnt Signaling Pathway

text: The Wnt/ $ \beta $-catenin signaling pathway is recognized as a crucial signal for the regulation of stem cell osteogenic differentiation and is strongly associated with the formation and regeneration of bone. The binding of canonical Wnt proteins to the Frizzled/LRP receptor leads to the activation of  $ \beta $-catenin, ultimately regulating the expression of osteogenic transcription factors. The combination of non-canonical Wnt proteins with the Frizzled receptor activates heterotrimeric G protein and regulates intracellular Ca $ ^{2+} $ signaling through  $ \beta $-catenin independent pathways. The osteogenic functions of some materials are closely related to this signaling pathway. It has been shown that a PCL nanofiber scaffold promotes human MSCs adhesion and proliferation and significantly increases the osteogenic differentiation potency of MSCs by consistently activating the Wnt/ $ \beta $-catenin and Smad3 pathways. $ ^{[178]} $ Silk braids with mechanical stiffness comparable to that of trabecular bone promoted human preosteoblast differentiation within their 3D microenvironment by the activation of Wnt/ $ \beta $-catenin signaling. $ ^{[179]} $ The Wnt/ $ \beta $-catenin pathway suppresses the adipogenic differentiation potential and alters the fate of cells from adipocytes to osteoblasts in adipose-derived MSCs. Chitosan-conjugated AuNPs increased the calcium deposit and the mRNA expression of osteogenic genes in human adipose-derived MSCs. Furthermore, immunofluorescence and Western blotting results indicate that the enhanced osteogenic effect is induced by the activation of the Wnt/ $ \beta $-catenin signaling pathway. $ ^{[180]} $

text: and skeletal development. Upon cell-cell contact, Notch ligands (Jagged and Delta) interact with Notch receptors (Notch1–4), leading to the cleavage of the Notch intracellular domain. Subsequently, the Notch intracellular domain translocates to the nucleus, and the binding of the Notch intracellular domain to the DNA-binding protein CBF1, Suppressor of Hairless, Lag-1 (CSL) activates the transcription of Notch target genes. Activation of Notch signaling in human bone marrow MSCs stimulates early osteogenic differentiation while suppressing mature osteoblast formation by inhibiting Runx2 activity. Notch2 signaling modulates osteogenic differentiation initiated by BMP and Wnt signaling pathways. Overexpressed Notch intracellular domain inhibits BMP2-mediated Runx2 transcriptional activity and the Wnt-dependent osteogenesis pathway. $ ^{[181]} $ Silk fibroin, a core structural protein, can be used as an osteogenic biomaterial. Silk fibroin facilitates osteoblast differentiation into multipotent cells by suppressing the Notch pathway. $ ^{[182]} $ BCP ceramics increased ALP activity and the expression of osteogenesis (OPN/Col/Runx2) in bone marrow-derived stem cells compared with BCP ceramics with Notch signaling inhibitors and control groups (Figure 8C). Histological and histomorphometric analyses demonstrated that the Notch signaling inhibitor delayed bone formation. These findings indicated that the Notch pathway plays an important role in BCP-induced osteogenesis. $ ^{[183]} $

text: 4.2.4. MAPK Signaling Pathway

raw_observation.text: 4.2.4. MAPK Signaling Pathway

marker_signature.normalized_text: 4.2.4. MAPK Signaling Pathway

text: The mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) family comprises extracellular regulated kinase (ERK), p38, and c-Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK), which are associated with signal transduction in osteoblasts. The MAPK signaling pathway regulates cell proliferation, adhesion, migration, and differentiation in response to external stimuli. Several types of mechanical stimuli, such as compressive force, mechanical stretching, and fluid shear stress, have been reported to enhance osteogenesis through the p38/MAPK pathway. Studies have shown that the MAPK pathway is involved in biomaterial-induced osteogenesis. Akermanite bioceramics ( $ Ca_{2}MgSi_{2}O_{7} $) can promote cell growth, increase ALP activity, and the expression of osteogenic genes and angiogenic factors, while inhibiting the expression of osteoclastogenic factors by activating the ERK, P38, AKT, and STAT3 pathways. $ ^{[184]} $ BGs composed of calcium silicate facilitate osteoblast proliferation and differentiation by activating the MAPK pathway via the release of Si and Ca ions. $ ^{[185]} $ BMDMs cultured on Ti with low surface potential are more inclined polarize towards anti-inflammatory phenotype (M2) in vitro and in vivo. Whole gene expression analysis reveals that inhibition of the PI3K-Akt-mTOR signaling axis is responsible for the immune regulation ability of Ti with low surface potential (Figure 8D). $ ^{[186]} $ Bioactive ions from  $ \beta $-calcium silicate/poly(D, L-lactide-co-glycolide) composite scaffolds facilitate cell proliferation, ALP activity, calcium deposit, and stimulate osteogenic differentiation and angiogenesis through the activation of the MAPK and PI3K/AKT pathways. $ ^{[187]} $ RNA-sequencing suggests that the osteogenic exosomes induce the osteogenic differentiation by using their cargos to activate the PI3K/Akt and MAPK signaling pathways (Figure 8E). $ ^{[101]} $

Several types of mechanical stimuli, such as compressive force, mechanical stretching, and fluid shear stress, have been reported to enhance osteogenesis through the p38/MAPK pathway. Studies have shown that the MAPK pathway is involved in biomaterial-induced osteogenesis. Akermanite bioceramics ( $ Ca_{2}MgSi_{2}O_{7} $) can promote cell growth, increase ALP activity, and the expression of osteogenic genes and angiogenic factors, while inhibiting the expression of osteoclastogenic factors by activating the ERK, P38, AKT, and STAT3 pathways. $ ^{[184]} $ BGs composed of calcium silicate facilitate osteoblast proliferation and differentiation by activating the MAPK pathway via the release of Si and Ca ions. $ ^{[185]} $ BMDMs cultured on Ti with low surface potential are more inclined polarize towards anti-inflammatory phenotype (M2) in vitro and in vivo. Whole gene expression analysis reveals that inhibition of the PI3K-Akt-mTOR signaling axis is responsible for the immune regulation ability of Ti with low surface potential (Figure 8D). $ ^{[186]} $ Bioactive ions from  $ \beta $-calcium silicate/poly(D, L-lactide-co-glycolide) composite scaffolds facilitate cell proliferation, ALP activity, calcium deposit, and stimulate osteogenic differentiation and angiogenesis through the activation of the MAPK and PI3K/AKT pathways. $ ^{[187]} $ RNA-sequencing suggests that the osteogenic exosomes induce the osteogenic differentiation by using their cargos to activate the PI3K/Akt and MAPK signaling pathways (Figure 8E). $ ^{[101]} $

The MAPK signaling pathway regulates cell proliferation, adhesion, migration, and differentiation in response to external stimuli. Several types of mechanical stimuli, such as compressive force, mechanical stretching, and fluid shear stress, have been reported to enhance osteogenesis through the p38/MAPK pathway. Studies have shown that the MAPK pathway is involved in biomaterial-induced osteogenesis. Akermanite bioceramics ( $ Ca_{2}MgSi_{2}O_{7} $) can promote cell growth, increase ALP activity, and the expression of osteogenic genes and angiogenic factors, while inhibiting the expression of osteoclastogenic factors by activating the ERK, P38, AKT, and STAT3 pathways. $ ^{[184]} $ BGs composed of calcium silicate facilitate osteoblast proliferation and differentiation by activating the MAPK pathway via the release of Si and Ca ions. $ ^{[185]} $ BMDMs cultured on Ti with low surface potential are more inclined polarize towards anti-inflammatory phenotype (M2) in vitro and in vivo. Whole gene expression analysis reveals that inhibition of the PI3K-Akt-mTOR signaling axis is responsible for the immune regulation ability of Ti with low surface potential (Figure 8D). $ ^{[186]} $ Bioactive ions from  $ \beta $-calcium silicate/poly(D, L-lactide-co-glycolide) composite scaffolds facilitate cell proliferation, ALP activity, calcium deposit, and stimulate osteogenic differentiation and angiogenesis through the activation of the MAPK and PI3K/AKT pathways. $ ^{[187]} $ RNA-sequencing suggests that the osteogenic exosomes induce the osteogenic differentiation by using their cargos to activate the PI3K/Akt and MAPK signaling pathways (Figure 8E). $ ^{[101]} $

text: In addition to the aforementioned pathways, bone development and homeostasis involve several other pathways that play an indispensable role in the osteogenic differentiation of undifferentiated and osteoprogenitor cells. For example, YAP/TAZ signaling, integrin signaling, calcium and phosphate signaling. Fast “dynamic bridges” to construct viscoelastic gelatin methacryloyl (GelMA)-based hydrogels, the viscous dissipation of hydrogels activated YAP by stabilizing integrin  $ \beta_1 $, and further promoted nuclear translocation of smad2/3 and  $ \beta $-catenin to enhance chondrogenesis and osteogenesis (Figure 8F). $ ^{[188]} $ NF- $ \kappa $B signaling, and Hedgehog signaling have been proven to regulate osteogenic differentiation in stem cells. Therefore, elucidating the mechanisms through which biomaterial properties modulate cellular responses and molecular events during bone regeneration is critical for designing superior bone biomaterials.

text: 5. Conclusions and Outlook

marker_signature.normalized_text: 5. Conclusions and Outlook

text: Herein, we summarized several biomaterials with unique biofunctions, bioactive compounds, 3D bioprinting techniques, and physical stimuli-responsive strategies for bone repair and regeneration. Among the materials, metals still need more research regarding their biocompatibility, biodegradability, and corrosion properties in vivo, as well as clinical evaluations, while ceramic biomaterials predominantly applied to fabricate bone scaffolds need to be combined with metals or polymers to obtain a suitable degradation rate and mechanical strength. Natural polymers have drawbacks such as low mechanical strength, high production cost, batch inconsistency, and immunogenicity, while synthetic polymers have limitations such as a lack of bioactivity, slow degradation, and harmful degradation products. These challenges have motivated biomaterial designers to develop new composites. Composites can enhance bioactivity and integrate the desirable properties of different materials to fully mimic the characteristics of natural bone.

text: 3D printing technology is a novel strategy in precision medicine that can produce bone implants using multiple materials based on digital models. With advances in 3D printing technology, novel composite materials with superior properties are rapidly emerging and being applied in clinical trials. 3D printing technology has great potential for personalized and accurate bone repair. Moreover, 3D bioprinting can precisely control the microstructure of the scaffolds. 3D printing can be used to customize scaffolds according to individual needs and to provide a suitable environment for cell adhesion and bone conduction. With the interdisciplinary development of 3D printing technology, 3D-printed bone scaffolds are expected to have excellent application prospects in bone repair and regeneration. In addition to 3D printing technology, physical stimuli-responsive strategies have received extensive attention. Physical stimuli-responsive biomaterials can use external triggers, such as light irradiation, electric and magnetic fields, ultrasound, or other mechanical stimuli, to achieve better therapeutic and regenerative outcomes. After eliminating severe infection, residual tumor tissue, or other bone diseases in a short and effective manner, a therapeutic biomaterial should promote cell attachment. Physical stimuli-responsive materials can release bioactive or

text: Despite the promising results presented herein, the literature primarily focuses on the development of scaffolds using advanced biomaterials, but lacks sufficient researches on the use of biomaterial-cell systems for bone repair and regeneration. Additionally, detailed protocols are required for the extraction, culturing, and seeding of stem cells on scaffolds to create tissues comparable to natural bone in terms of quality and stability. The application of biomaterials for bone regeneration still requires a thorough understanding of the complex molecular mechanisms involved in osteogenesis induction and faces several challenges that need to be addressed in future investigations.

text: Shengguang Chen is a clinical doctor at Gongli Hospital of Shanghai Pudong New Area. He has been engaged in clinical research on endocrine and metabolic diseases. He received his Ph.D. degree in medical from Tongji University. Currently, his research interests mainly focus on the diagnosis and treatment of metabolic diseases, especially bone metabolic diseases, including osteoporosis, hyperparathyroidism, osteomalacia, osteogenesis imperfecta, osteoarthritis, etc.

text: Bei Ma is a professor at Tongji Hospital affiliated to Tongji University. She obtained her Ph.D. at Second Military Medical University, China, in 2008. She did her post-doctoral work at Geoffrey Burnstock's laboratory at University College of London. She is a fellow of Chinese Association for Physiological Science and a fellow of Chinese Neuroscience Society. Currently, her research focus on the underlying physiological and pathological mechanisms of nerve damage and repair after spinal cord injury.

text: Rongrong Zhu is a professor at Tongji University, affiliated Tongji hospital and Spinal Cord Injury Research Institute. She focused on the field "Regenerative Medicine and Nanomaterials" to regulate the microenvironment of injury site and promote the tissue regeneration through the design, optimization, synthesis of nanomaterials. She obtained her Ph.D. at Tongjii University, China, in 2007. She went on postdoctoral research at Stony Brook University in the United States in 2009. She is a member of neuroprotection group in Neuro-regeneration and Repair Committee of Chinese Research Hospitals Society, also a director of Shanghai Society for Biochemistry and Molecular Biology.
